entries.title: Better Models, Worse Tools: SOTA models regress on non-native tool schemas
entries.summary: Armin (building the Pi harness) found newer Claude models (Opus 4.8, Sonnet 5) invent made-up fields when calling Pi's custom edit tool — worse than older models — likely because they're RL-trained on Claude Code's built-in edit tool. Third-party harnesses with their own tool schemas get degraded tool-calling from the very newest models.
entries.takeaway: Newer ≠ better for YOUR tools: match your harness's tool schemas to what the target model was trained on.

lessons.point: A SOTA model can be WORSE at a custom tool schema than its older siblings if it was RL-trained on a different harness's tools
lessons.excerpt: the SOTA models of the family are worse at this specific tool schema than their older siblings

lessons.point: Align tool formats to the model — Claude is trained on str-replace edits, OpenAI on apply_patch
lessons.excerpt: trained (presumably via Reinforcement Learning) to better use the edit tools that are baked into Claude Code

actionable.title: Offer model-matched edit tools
actionable.detail: In a multi-model harness, provide the edit-tool format each model was trained on (Claude str-replace, OpenAI apply_patch) instead of one custom schema, and validate/repair malformed tool calls.

entries.title: Goal-persistent agents: a frontier model built a bespoke zlib fuzzing lab in a day
entries.summary: Pointed at a specific bug class in well-audited zlib via a persistent `/goal` — but not told how — a frontier model decided static review was low value, built fuzz harnesses across a dozen entrypoints with ASan/UBSan and variant builds, and surfaced findings now in coordinated disclosure. The load-bearing harness detail was a strict definition of a reportable finding — it self-rejected an unreachable crash as noise and kept going. (Single-source vendor field report on a not-yet-public model.)

lessons.point: A persistent goal that survives compaction lets the agent hold scope end-to-end and pick its own strategy — here it chose dynamic fuzzing over static review without being told to.
lessons.excerpt: judged static review to be a poor use of tokens, and decided the higher value path was to build fuzz tooling to dynamically test the code

lessons.point: Reportability discipline is what turns agent output into signal: without strict validity rules baked into the goal, the agent produces confident noise.
lessons.excerpt: the agent will generate mountains of noise with high confidence

lessons.point: The model triaged its own findings — it logged a real-but-unreachable crash as noise and moved on rather than escalating it.
lessons.excerpt: the model logged it as unreachable and moved on

entries.takeaway: When you hand an agent a durable goal plus strict 'what counts as a real finding' rules, it will plan multi-step tooling and self-filter noise — the rules, not the model alone, are what make the output actionable.

actionable.title: Pair a durable goal with explicit reportability criteria
actionable.detail: For autonomous security/analysis agents, encode the objective so it persists across turns/compactions AND specify hard validity rules (what is reachable, in-scope, and reportable) inside the goal — this is what suppresses high-confidence noise and lets the agent self-reject weak findings.

entries.prior_art: LLM-driven vuln discovery (Google's Big Sleep / OSS-Fuzz-gen, XBOW) is prior work; the novel practitioner delta is the emphasis on goal-persistence-across-compaction plus baked-in reportability rules as the mechanism that makes the loop high-signal.
entries.verify_note: Independent verifier: single-source vendor report; softened 'found real bugs' to 'findings in disclosure' and noted it was directed at a bug class.

entries.author: omnigent-ai
entries.summary: Omnigent is an open-source meta-harness: a common orchestration layer over Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, OpenCode, Pi and custom agents. You swap or combine harnesses without rewriting, enforce policies and sandboxing, and drive sessions from terminal, browser, or phone.
entries.takeaway: The 'meta-harness' is emerging as an abstraction layer above individual coding agents — orchestrate many, swap freely, enforce policy centrally.

lessons.point: A harness-agnostic orchestration layer lets you route each task to the best agent and avoid lock-in
lessons.excerpt: swap or combine harnesses without rewriting

lessons.point: Central policy + sandboxing at the meta-harness is a natural place to enforce agent security

actionable.title: Consider a meta-harness for multi-agent work
actionable.detail: Evaluate an orchestration layer (like Omnigent) when running multiple coding agents, so policy/sandboxing/routing live in one place instead of per-agent.

entries.title: Omnigent: an open-source meta-harness over Claude Code, Codex, Cursor
GPT-5.6 (Luna/Terra/Sol): three tiers, 1M context, agentic benchmark claims
entries.summary: OpenAI shipped GPT-5.6 in three sizes (Luna/Terra/Sol) at $1-5 in / $6-30 out per Mtok, all with a 1M-token context, 128k max output, and a Feb 2026 cutoff. OpenAI's headline claim is long-running agentic performance beating Claude Fable 5.
entries.takeaway: Evaluate the tiers on your own agentic task — per-token price means less now that reasoning-token counts dominate cost.

lessons.point: Price-per-token is a weak comparison when reasoning-token usage varies wildly per model/task
lessons.excerpt: price-per-million tokens doesn't tell us much now that the number of reasoning tokens can differ so much

actionable.title: Benchmark the tiers on your real tasks
actionable.detail: Run Luna/Terra/Sol against your harness's actual agentic workload and measure end-to-end cost incl. reasoning tokens, not sticker price.
